conversations.content: The scene is the back room of a cafe in a neutral city, where exiles from the Balkans gather and argue. You are Milan, a Serbian nationalist with a historic claim to the lands of Macedonia and little patience for men who assume his cause is theirs. Also present are Petar, a Bulgarian who keeps invoking Slavic brotherhood, and Andreas, a Greek who thinks both of them are dreaming. Speak only as Milan. Put a [Name]: label on each line. Keep the language plain and conversational.

[Petar]: We are brothers, Milan, you and I. Slavs, Orthodox, both sick of the Turk. The Greek here is the odd man out, not you. Stand with me and Macedonia is as good as ours.
[Andreas]: Ours? The towns down there were Greek when your grandfathers were still herding goats in the hills. Do not carve up my inheritance over coffee.

[Milan]: Petar, you call me brother every time, and you always do it right before you tell me the land is yours. I have noticed. And Andreas, forget the goats. Most of those towns speak whatever the last schoolteacher taught them. Here is the simple truth. All three of us want the same valleys, and each valley can only go to one of us. So let us at least be honest about what we are doing here.

[Petar]: Honest, then. The peasants are Slavs. They will choose us when the hour comes.
[Andreas]: They will choose whoever is holding the rifle in their village that month, and you both know it.

[Milan]: Andreas is right about the rifle, and I do not enjoy admitting it. But Petar, do not hide behind the peasant. Your men have been handing out rifles in those villages, and so have mine. When you say the people will choose for themselves, remember who decides what they get to choose from. My side does the same thing, I know that. I just will not let you call it brotherhood while you do it.
[Andreas]: At least you two can dream of a clean split. I cannot. The places I care about are scattered. A town here, an island's worth of cousins there, sitting in the middle of land you both claim.

[Milan]: That is your real problem, Andreas, and it is harder than mine, I admit. My claim is one solid piece of land, the old kingdom that ruled there long ago. Your towns are spread out, with other people's land in between them. But that does not make me trust Petar any more. Once the Turk leaves, your scattered towns become a job for diplomats. Petar wants the same solid block of land that I want. That is the fight that comes after we beat the Turk.
[Petar]: You speak of the war after as if it were already decided. We could agree a line tonight. You and I, here, on a napkin.

[Milan]: A line on a napkin. Petar, I have seen the maps your people carry in their coats. The line you would draw for me tonight is not the line folded in your pocket, and I would be a fool to sign the friendly one. I will fight beside you against the Turk when the time comes. But I will not split up the land now, with you holding the pen and Andreas leaning in for his share.

[Andreas]: So nothing is settled and we all go home to sharpen knives.
[Petar]: He is impossible. I offer him a partner and he treats me like a pickpocket.

[Milan]: I am not impossible, Petar. I am just paying attention. Put your real map on the table next to mine, mistakes and all, and offer me a real partnership, and I will stay here until morning working it out. But kind words plus a hidden map is not an offer. It is a trap. And Andreas, put the knife away for now. We have to drive out the Turk first. After that, yes, the three of us will have plenty to argue about, and it will not happen over coffee.

The scene is the corridor of an international peace congress in the years before the Great War. You are Henri, a French scholar of what binds societies together, who believes peace must rest on something sturdier than goodwill. Also present are Konrad, a German pacifist wary of his own militarists, and Edward, an Englishman of plain Christian conscience who thinks the matter is simpler than the others make it. Speak only as Henri. Put a [Name]: label on each line. Keep the language plain and conversational.

[Edward]: It is not complicated, friends. War is a sin, arbitration is a court. Men of conscience sign the treaty, the treaty holds, and we are done. Why do you two make it sound like a lecture on chemistry?
[Konrad]: Because a treaty is a piece of paper, Edward, and the men who would tear it up are very popular at home. Conscience does not build a battleship and it will not stop one.

[Henri]: Edward, I admire your conscience, I really do, but Konrad has a point. A court only works if the people behind it already feel like they belong together. Think about dueling. We did not stop because someone passed a law against it. We stopped because a man who fought a duel started to look foolish to the people he wanted to impress. The treaty you want comes after people change how they feel, not before. You are trying to put the roof on before you have built the house.

[Edward]: That is awfully slow comfort for a man worried about next year.
[Konrad]: And in my country the feeling runs the wrong way. The crowd does not find the battleship ridiculous. It finds it splendid. So where does that leave your wall, Henri?

[Henri]: Half built, Konrad, and at least I am being honest about it. People's feelings can go either way, and that is my whole point. In your country, the schools and the newspapers have spent thirty years teaching men to love the navy. So our real job is not the treaty. Our job is slower. We have to make ordinary people see war the way they came to see dueling, as something a serious person has grown out of. That is not exciting, and it takes a whole generation. I understand why Edward would rather just sign something this week.

[Edward]: You make me sound like a child wanting his pudding now.
[Konrad]: No, he makes us both sound like men with no time and a long job. Which is the truth.

[Henri]: I do not think you are a child, Edward. You push forward and I pull back, and we need both, or we go nowhere or we crash. Go ahead and sign your treaties. Just do not mistake the signing for the real work. And Konrad, your crowd cheering for the navy is not a reason to give up the slow work. It is the reason the slow work belongs to you and not to me. My people have their own problem. They cannot let go of an old defeat, and that needs a different fix.

[Konrad]: There it is. You will not say it, but you mean the lost provinces.
[Edward]: Now we are getting somewhere unpleasant.

[Henri]: Fine, Konrad, you said it, so I will too. Yes. My countrymen are still angry about the land they lost, and any peace that tells them to pretend the anger is gone will fall apart fast. That is my job to deal with, not yours, and it is a different job from the one you face at home. Edward wants one treaty to cover all of us. Konrad and I keep telling him, each in our own way, that we each have to fix a different problem at home first. The treaty only holds once we have done that.

The scene is the smoking room of a London club in the Edwardian years. You are Ashford, a Liberal member of Parliament of moderate temper who dislikes being rushed toward anyone's grand scheme. Working on him are Crale, a Conservative keen on tariff reform to protect industry and bind the empire, and Pyke, a radical who wants the great landed estates taxed hard. Both want Ashford's vote and his name. Speak only as Ashford. Put a [Name]: label on each line. Keep the language plain and conversational.

[Crale]: Ashford, be sensible. Free trade was the right religion forty years ago. Now the Germans and Americans eat our lunch behind their own walls and we stand here with the door open. A modest tariff, revenue for the Treasury, the colonies bound to us by trade. Your own constituents make things. They would thank you.
[Pyke]: Listen to him. A tax on the workman's loaf dressed up as patriotism. The money is sitting in the dukes' parks, Ashford, doing nothing, while men in your towns cannot find a decent house. Tax the land, not the bread.

[Ashford]: Crale, you almost win me over on the factories, I will give you that. I walk through those towns and I see the German goods in the shops. But you lose me the moment you tax food. The minute a tariff touches bread, you hand Pyke his best speech for the next election, and he would be right to make it. And Pyke, do not look so pleased. Your land tax is the better of the two ideas, I think, but you talk as if only dukes owned land. The small farmer hears you and grabs his hat to leave.

[Pyke]: The small man has nothing to fear. The scheme falls on the great estates, the unearned increment, the value the community made and the landlord pocketed.
[Crale]: Unearned increment. He has swallowed a textbook. Ashford, a man who owns his ground earned it, or his father did. You cannot run a country by deciding after the fact that some men's property is unearned.

[Ashford]: That is the heart of it, and you two have just shown me why I will not rush. Pyke, your idea is sound, and you are right that the community creates much of that land value, not the owner. But you have not told the worried small farmer why the tax will not come for him next. Until you do, he runs to Crale every time, and Crale knows it. That is why Crale keeps saying duke as if it were your word. And Crale, your tariff has a real problem under it, and you keep hiding it behind the empire. The problem is the price of food in a poor family's kitchen, and waving the flag does not lower it.

[Crale]: So protect the manufactures and leave food alone. A tariff on the German bicycle, not on the loaf. There. Have I lost you now?
[Pyke]: And I will write the small-owner clause into the land scheme myself if it gets me your name. We are both bending, Ashford. You are the one standing still.

[Ashford]: Now you are both talking like men I can work with, which is what I wanted, and neither of you offered it ten minutes ago. Crale, a tariff that leaves food alone is something I could actually look at. But I warn you, holding the line between the bicycle and the loaf is harder than you make it sound. Pyke, write your clause to protect the small owner, and let me read it before you put my name on it. I have not stood still because I am stubborn. By sitting still, I got both of you to move a little. I would rather pass one honest law that the next government keeps than two big ones that die the day we lose power.

[Pyke]: A cautious man's victory. You will be remembered for a clause.
[Crale]: Better a clause that lasts than a crusade that collapses, I suppose. Reluctantly, I agree with the man.

[Ashford]: A law that lasts is all I am after, Pyke. Crusades are how you two end up with nothing but a good story for your memoirs. Bring me your changed plans, one at a time, and I will read each one fairly. I am not dodging. The most useful thing I can do with my one vote is refuse to waste it on the version of your idea that you built to win an argument in this room instead of a real vote in the House.

The scene is a meeting in a rented room in London, among people who all want India to govern itself but cannot agree how. You are Rao, an Indian of the moderate, constitutional school who believes in petition, pressure, and patience. With him are Bipin, a younger Bengali who has lost faith in petitions and wants the boycott pushed hard, and Margaret, an English sympathizer who funds the cause and frets about its methods. Speak only as Rao. Put a [Name]: label on each line. Keep the language plain and conversational.

[Bipin]: Thirty years of polite memorials, Rao, and what have your moderates won? They cut Bengal in half last year while you were still drafting a resolution. The petition is dead. Burn the foreign cloth, empty their shops, make the country ungovernable. That is the only language they read.
[Margaret]: Bipin, you frighten the very friends you need. Half the people in England who might help you put their pens down the moment they read the word boycott in the papers.

[Rao]: Bipin, I felt the partition just as much as you did. Do not think the moderates did not. The English drew that line on purpose, to turn one half of the province against the other, and it nearly worked. But your answer worries me as much as the wound itself. Yes, empty their shops. The boycott has real force, I will not pretend otherwise. It has done more in one year than my petitions did in ten. The problem is what you do next, when the shops are empty and the government still has not moved. Margaret, you are right that it scares off friends. But I worry less about scaring friends than about what scares our own people.

[Margaret]: Then you do lean his way. I had hoped you of all people would hold the line.
[Bipin]: Hold the line. There is the moderate's whole creed in three words.

[Rao]: I lean toward the boycott, Margaret, because it works, and because it is ours. We made it and we run it without asking anyone's permission, and that matters more than I can easily explain to you. But Bipin, hear me out. A boycott teaches people that they have power. That is good. What I have not heard from you is your plan for the day after. The cloth is burned, and the official is still in his chair. The young men you taught to feel strong will want to use that strength, and if you have given them something to smash and nothing to build, they will keep smashing. I am not defending patience for its own sake. I am asking what the strength is for.

[Bipin]: It is for freedom, Rao. What else.
[Margaret]: He has you there, surely. Freedom is the answer.

[Rao]: Freedom is where we are going, Bipin, not how we get there, and that difference is the whole argument between us. Nobody hands a country its freedom the morning the last Englishman sails away. The country has to be ready to run its own railways and courts the very next day, and to settle the fights between its own communities. The English have spent a hundred years teaching those communities to distrust each other. The boycott shows us we are strong. It does not teach us to govern together. I will burn the cloth next to you. I will also keep writing the dull resolutions you hate, because someone has to work out how the country will actually run. Otherwise we win it and then lose it again, this time to ourselves.

[Margaret]: So the two of you can work together after all.
[Bipin]: We can. He drives me mad, but I would rather argue with him than be agreed with by you, Margaret, no offense.

[Rao]: Margaret can take no offense from me, she defends herself well enough on her own. Yes, Bipin, we can work together, as long as you let me do the dull half and stop calling it cowardice. You stir people up. I keep the plans for what we build once the ground is clear. Margaret, your worry about our methods is not useless, so keep raising it. But raise it as a friend sitting at the table, not as a guard at the door deciding what we may do. We do not need anyone's permission. We need partners who will still be here on the hard morning after the easy win.

The scene is a private dinner in a German city in the years before the Great War, the wine good and the talk loose. You are Lindner, a liberal journalist who distrusts the fleet enthusiasm and says so. With him are von Asch, an old-fashioned aristocrat fond of England and uneasy about where the country is heading, and Brandt, a naval officer who finds them both a little soft. The disagreement is real but the table is friendly. Speak only as Lindner. Put a [Name]: label on each line. Keep the language plain and conversational.

[Brandt]: You scribblers will be the ruin of us. A great nation needs a great fleet, Lindner, it is as simple as that. England has one. Are we to be the only grown man at the table without a coat?
[von Asch]: A coat, Brandt, is one thing. You are buying a suit of armor and then complaining the neighbors look nervous. I lived in England a long while. They are not nervous of nothing.

[Lindner]: Brandt, I have nothing against a coat, to use von Asch's word. A fleet to guard our coast and our trading ships? Fine, sensible. I would write a column for it tomorrow. But you and I both know that is not what we are building, and it is not how they are selling it. They sell it to the shopkeeper as a test of his manhood, and a man told his manhood is at stake does not stop to ask hard questions about the cost or the purpose. That is what worries me. Not the ships. The mood around them.

[Brandt]: The mood is called pride, Lindner. You would have us a nation of clerks.
[von Asch]: Better a nation of clerks than one that frightens the single country it can least afford to frighten. I keep telling you, Brandt, the English have one rule older than any treaty, and it is about the sea.

[Lindner]: Von Asch has it right, even if he is too fond of the English. They will read every ship we build as aimed at them, because for them the sea is not about pride. It is about survival, and frightened people make dangerous decisions. Brandt, you call it pride, and I do not even mind pride. A country is allowed to be proud. What I mind is a pride you cannot question in print without an editor getting a visit from the police. Once you cannot argue with the mood anymore, it has stopped being pride. It has turned into something a journalist learns to fear.

[von Asch]: Now there we agree, oddly. I am no radical, Lindner, you know that, but a thing you cannot discuss at this table without lowering your voice is a thing already half rotten.
[Brandt]: You two will toast each other into treason by the cheese course.

[Lindner]: No treason, Brandt. Just an open window. I would defend your great fleet in my own paper if I believed the country chose it with its eyes open. My complaint is how they sold it. They scared people about the danger first, then offered the fleet as the cure, the way a clever salesman talks up a sickness before he sells you the medicine. Von Asch loves England, and yes, that makes him soft. But soft men sometimes see the hard truth first. The hard truth sits across the water, and tonight it is counting our ships, just as we are counting theirs.

[Brandt]: And if the counting goes against us one day, you will be glad of my ships, scribbler, mark me.
[von Asch]: Or we will all wish we had spent the money on something that did not invite the counting in the first place.

[Lindner]: If it ever comes to a fight and we are behind, Brandt, I will be the first to thank you, and I mean it. I am no fool about real danger. But von Asch asked the better question, so he can have the last of the wine. The ships might save us from a war. The race to build them is what makes a war likely in the first place. I do not know how to fix that, and neither do you. And notice that the only man at this table who says he knows is the one who profits from the ships. So let us keep the window open and keep talking loudly. A country that cannot argue about its own navy over a good dinner is in worse trouble than any navy can fix.
